---
author: Luke Elson, luke.elson@reading.ac.uk
title: The Repeating Puzzle of the Self-Torturer
lang: en-GB
format:
  pdf:
    papersize: a4paper
    pdf-engine: xelatex
bibliography: ../../../zotero.bib
---

> Suppose that there is a medical device that enables doctors to apply
> electric current to the body in increments so tiny that the patient
> cannot feel them. The device has 1001 settings: 0 (off) and 1
> ... 1000. Suppose someone (call him the self-torturer) agrees to
> have the device, in some conveniently portable form, attached to him
> [...]  The device is initially set to 0. At the start of each week
> he is allowed a period of free experimentation in which he may try
> out and compare different settings, after which the the dial is
> returned to its previous position. At any other time, he has only
> two options -- to stay put or to advance the dial one setting. But
> he may advance only one step each week, and he may *never*
> retreat. *At each advance he gets $10,000.* [@Quinn1990,
> p. 79. Emphasis in original.]


> With every death she took on a little more pain... an aching tooth
> here, a stabbing feeling in her gut there, a persistent headache,
> what felt like a trapped nerve in one hip ... a thousand almost
> infinitesimal little pangs and stings and sprains and strains and
> ulcers and chafings, either adding incrementally to some
> established hurt or starting a fresh site. ... No single ache
> dominated, and even when taken together the sum of them was not
> utterly debilitating, but they all nagged, all had their effect,
> filling her days... [@Banks2011, pp. 471--2]


Nonsegmentation.
:    When faced with a certain series of choices, the rational
    self-torturer must choose to stop turning the dial before the last
    setting; whereas in any isolated choice, she must (or at least
    may) choose to turn the dial. [@TenenbaumRaffman2012, p. 98]

# The Repeating Model

Stipulatively, a *life-altering* pain increase is determinately not worth it for $100,000.

It's indeterminate whether a
  life-altering pain increase happens after 8, 9, or 10 dial turns.

At stage $n$, ST has undergone $\lfloor n/s \rfloor$ life-altering
  pain increases, where $s$ is indeterminately 8, 9, or 10.

Utility from money is linear in ten thousand dollars: $u_{m}(n)=n$

Since a life-altering pain increase outweighs $100,000, let's assume
  it's worth -11 utils: $u_{p}(n)=-11\lfloor n/s \rfloor$

At many points, it's indeterminate whether ST prefers to turn the dial.

Over every stretch of 10 dial-turns the lines all decline. In 10 turns ST gains 10 utils from turning the dial but loses 11 from a life-altering pain increase.

![The final model -- $u(n)=n-11\lfloor n/s \rfloor$ for different values of $s$](./new-utilities.svg)

# References
